replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across both M62 and N62 engines. Reconditioned units are rare — specialists typically rebuild existing units. All pricing is indicative; actual costs vary by mileage, condition and workshop location.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X5 V8 engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent BMW specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The E53's engine bay is tight; engine-out work is often required for major repairs.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW X5 4.6is / 4.8is — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replacement often £4,500–£7,500 — assess vehicle value carefully

buyingChecks.title: Buying This BMW X5 4.6is / 4.8is? Check These First
buyIf: * Any 4.8is with documented valve stem seal history (replaced)
* 4.6is with documented timing chain guide history (replaced)
* Cooling system proactively replaced (water pump, radiator, hoses)
* Full service history with regular oil changes (specialist maintained)
avoidIf: * 4.6is with no timing chain history — the risk is real and expensive
* 4.8is with blue smoke on startup — valve stem seals are almost certainly failing
* Any evidence of coolant loss or overheating — cooling system risk
* Poor service history — both V8s are unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — these engines can hide expensive problems
* Rust — E53s are valuable; body condition is as important as the engine

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 4.6is and 4.8is are BMW's high-performance E53 X5s — V8-powered SUVs with genuine sporting credentials. The 4.6is M62 is a classic V8 with chain guide vulnerability and VANOS issues; the 4.8is N62 is more powerful but has the valve stem seal oil consumption problem. Both are high-maintenance specialist engines. Buy the newest, lowest-mileage example you can afford with documented history, budget for preventative maintenance, and enjoy one of BMW's most iconic SUVs.

cta.label: → Compare BMW X5 4.6is / 4.8is engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X5 4.6is / 4.8is (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Any rattle? M62 chain rattle is metallic; N62 should be smooth but check for blue smoke
* Blue Smoke — N62: any smoke on startup? Valve stem seals are the main issue
* Oil Consumption — N62: monitor between services; 1L per 1,000 miles is a warning sign
* Service History — Check the year to identify which engine — 2002–2003 = M62, 2004–2006 = N62
* Timing Chain History — M62: guides and tensioner replaced? (critical)
* Cooling System — Any coolant loss? Water pump, radiator and hoses should be recent
* VANOS Performance — M62: check low-end torque; hesitation indicates VANOS seal wear
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying

items.question: How much does a BMW X5 4.6is / 4.8is engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned BMW X5 V8 engine costs £4,500–£7,500 supply only, varying by generation: M62 4.6is £4,000–£6,500, N62 4.8is £5,000–£8,000. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500. These are specialist-only replacements — the V8 requires BMW V8 expertise.

items.question: Is the BMW X5 4.6is / 4.8is reliable?
items.answer: That depends on which engine your X5 has. The M62 (4.6is, 2002–2003) is a classic V8 but has timing chain guide failure risk — this is the defining issue. The N62 (4.8is, 2004–2006) has valve stem seal failure (blue smoke, oil consumption) as the defining weakness. Both are high-maintenance specialist engines. Neither is cheap to run.

items.question: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 4.6is / 4.8is?
items.answer: M62: timing chain guide failure (cold-start rattle, £2,000–£4,500), VANOS seal degradation (loss of low-end power, £600–£1,500), cooling system failure (£500–£1,500). N62: valve stem seal failure (blue smoke, oil consumption, £2,500–£5,000), alternator bracket oil leak (£800–£1,800), cooling system failure (£500–£1,500).

items.question: Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 4.6is / 4.8is engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: E53 X5 values range from £4,000–£15,000 depending on condition, mileage and specification. A £4,500–£7,500 reconditioned engine represents 30–80% of vehicle value — our 70% BMW scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained, low-mileage example, replacement is often justified. For a higher-mileage example with other issues, assess scrap value first. The 4.8is is generally more valuable and worth the investment.

items.question: How do I find my BMW X5 4.6is / 4.8is engine code?
items.answer: The X5 4.6is/4.8is has used two engines: M62B46 (2002–2003, 340bhp) and N62B48 (2004–2006, 355bhp). The 4.6is badge is the M62; the 4.8is badge is the N62. Check your VIN or registration year. The code is stamped on the engine block.

items.question: Can I repair the M62 timing chain issue, or does it need replacement?
items.answer: Yes — if caught early. Timing chain and guide replacement costs £2,000–£4,500 and is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement (£4,000–£6,500). The job requires engine removal on the E53, making it a major investment. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately — ignored chain wear can lead to catastrophic piston-valve contact.

items.question: Can I repair the N62 valve stem seal issue, or does it need replacement?
items.answer: Yes — the valve stem seals can be replaced using a specialist method that doesn't require removing the cylinder heads (some specialists can do it with the engine in situ using air pressure to hold the valves). This costs £2,500–£5,000 and is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement (£5,000–£8,000). At the first sign of blue smoke on startup, book an inspection.
